questions.stem: Fiscal policy refers to the use of which instruments to influence the economy?
options: Exchange rates and tariffs
Government spending and taxation
Interest rates and the money supply
Wages and prices set by firms
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy is the government's use of its spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand and the economy. Interest rates and the money supply are the tools of monetary policy.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Monetary policy is mainly concerned with controlling what?
options: Government spending on roads
Interest rates and the money supply
The minimum wage
The level of income tax
questions.explanation: Monetary policy works through the cost and availability of money and credit, chiefly by setting interest rates and influencing the money supply. Taxes and government spending belong to fiscal policy.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In most countries, which institution is mainly responsible for conducting monetary policy?
options: The central bank
The ministry of education
Trade unions
Commercial retailers
questions.explanation: Monetary policy is usually set by the central bank, often with operational independence. Fiscal policy, by contrast, is decided by the government and treasury through the budget.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An expansionary fiscal policy to fight a recession would typically involve what?
options: Higher government spending and lower taxes
Lower government spending and higher taxes
Higher interest rates
Selling government bonds to reduce the money supply
questions.explanation: To raise aggregate demand in a downturn, the government can spend more and tax less, boosting total spending. Cutting spending and raising taxes would be contractionary, and the bond and interest options are monetary tools.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A government runs a budget deficit when what is true?
options: It has no national debt
Its spending equals its revenue
Its spending exceeds its revenue in a given period
Its revenue exceeds its spending
questions.explanation: A budget deficit means government spending is greater than the revenue it raises (mainly from taxes) over the period. The shortfall is usually financed by borrowing, which adds to the national debt.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The national debt is best described as what?
options: The total stock of government borrowing accumulated over time
The value of the country's exports
The size of this year's budget deficit
The amount of money in circulation
questions.explanation: The national debt is the accumulated total that the government owes, built up from past deficits. The annual deficit is a flow that adds to this stock each year a deficit is run.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a direct tax?
options: Income tax
Value added tax (VAT)
Import tariff
Excise duty on petrol
questions.explanation: A direct tax is levied on income or wealth and paid directly by the person or firm liable, such as income tax. VAT, excise duties, and tariffs are indirect taxes levied on spending.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A tax is described as 'progressive' when what happens as income rises?
options: The proportion of income paid in tax falls
The proportion of income paid in tax rises
The same proportion of income is paid at all income levels
No tax is paid at any income level
questions.explanation: Under a progressive tax, higher earners pay a larger share of their income in tax. A regressive tax takes a smaller share as income rises, and a proportional tax takes the same share at all levels.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: 'Automatic stabilisers' refer to what?
options: Interest rate changes by the central bank
New laws passed during every recession
Features of the budget, such as taxes and benefits, that dampen the cycle without new decisions
Fixed exchange rate agreements
questions.explanation: Automatic stabilisers are parts of the tax and benefit system that respond to the cycle on their own. In a slump, tax receipts fall and benefit payments rise, cushioning demand without any new policy action.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: To slow down an economy that is overheating with high inflation, a contractionary fiscal policy would do what?
options: Lower interest rates
Increase the money supply
Raise taxes and cut government spending
Cut taxes and raise government spending
questions.explanation: Contractionary fiscal policy reduces aggregate demand by raising taxes and cutting spending, which helps ease demand-pull inflation. The other options would either expand demand or are monetary tools.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: To stimulate a weak economy, an expansionary monetary policy would typically involve what?
options: Cutting government spending
Increasing income tax
Lowering interest rates
Raising interest rates
questions.explanation: Lower interest rates reduce the cost of borrowing and the reward for saving, encouraging consumption and investment. This raises aggregate demand. Tax and spending changes are fiscal, not monetary, tools.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the central bank wishes to reduce inflation, it is most likely to do what?
options: Lower its policy interest rate
Increase government spending
Cut income tax rates
Raise its policy interest rate
questions.explanation: Raising interest rates makes borrowing dearer and saving more attractive, cooling spending and easing inflationary pressure. Cutting rates would do the opposite, and the tax and spending options are fiscal measures.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a tool of monetary policy?
options: Paying unemployment benefits
Open market operations in government bonds
Changes to the rate of income tax
Building new public hospitals
questions.explanation: Open market operations, where the central bank buys or sells government bonds to change reserves and the money supply, are a standard monetary tool. The other options are forms of government spending or taxation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: 'Crowding out' refers to the idea that government borrowing to fund a deficit may do what?
options: Raise interest rates and reduce private sector investment
Eliminate the need for taxation
Always increase private investment
Lower the price level permanently
questions.explanation: If the government borrows heavily, it can push up interest rates as it competes for funds, discouraging private investment. This partial offset to fiscal expansion is known as crowding out.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A balanced budget occurs when what condition holds?
options: Government revenue equals government spending
Exports equal imports
The central bank sets interest rates to zero
Government spending is double its revenue
questions.explanation: A balanced budget means planned government revenue and spending are equal, so there is neither a deficit nor a surplus over the period. The trade balance and interest rates are separate matters.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Supply-side policies are aimed mainly at what?
options: Raising the economy's productive capacity and efficiency
Fixing the exchange rate
Increasing aggregate demand in the short run only
Raising the budget deficit
questions.explanation: Supply-side policies, such as improving education, cutting red tape, and reforming labour markets, try to increase potential output and efficiency. They shift long run aggregate supply rather than just demand.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a supply-side policy?
options: A temporary rise in unemployment benefits
Selling foreign currency reserves
Investment in education and worker training
An increase in the central bank's policy rate
questions.explanation: Spending on education and training raises workers' skills and productivity, expanding the economy's productive potential. That is a supply-side measure, unlike short term demand or monetary actions.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A 'time lag' problem in fiscal policy means what?
options: There is a delay between recognising a problem and the policy taking effect
The central bank controls all spending
Taxes can never be changed
Policy works instantly with no delay
questions.explanation: Fiscal policy suffers from recognition, decision, and implementation lags, so its effects arrive only after a delay. By the time policy bites, economic conditions may have changed, which can make fine tuning difficult.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Increasing the reserve requirement on commercial banks is a monetary action that will tend to do what?
options: Increase bank lending and loosen the money supply
Directly raise income tax revenue
Reduce bank lending and tighten the money supply
Have no effect on lending
questions.explanation: A higher reserve requirement forces banks to hold more reserves and lend less, shrinking credit creation and tightening the money supply. It is a contractionary monetary measure.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Both expansionary fiscal and expansionary monetary policy are classed as demand-side policies because they primarily aim to do what?
options: Influence the level of aggregate demand
Change the number of firms in each industry
Increase the country's gold reserves
Set the prices of individual goods
questions.explanation: Demand-side policies work by shifting aggregate demand. Fiscal policy does this through spending and taxes, monetary policy through interest rates and the money supply, but both target total spending.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which combination would most strongly stimulate aggregate demand?
options: Higher taxes together with higher interest rates
Higher taxes together with lower government spending
Lower taxes together with lower interest rates
Lower taxes together with higher interest rates
questions.explanation: Cutting taxes (expansionary fiscal) and cutting interest rates (expansionary monetary) both raise spending, so combining them gives the strongest boost to aggregate demand. The other mixes are tighter or offsetting.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A regressive tax, such as a flat sales tax on essential goods, has what effect?
options: It applies only to company profits
It takes a larger share of income from higher income households
It takes the same amount of money from everyone regardless of price
It takes a larger share of income from lower income households
questions.explanation: A regressive tax falls more heavily, in proportion to income, on the poor, because spending on essentials makes up a larger share of a low income. This is a common criticism of broad indirect taxes.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a government wants to reduce its budget deficit, which action is consistent with that aim?
options: Lowering the central bank's interest rate
Increasing transfer payments only
Increasing tax revenue or cutting public spending
Cutting taxes while raising spending
questions.explanation: A deficit is reduced by narrowing the gap between spending and revenue, that is by raising more tax or spending less. Cutting taxes while spending more would widen the deficit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: One argument for central bank independence in setting monetary policy is that it does what?
options: Fixes the exchange rate permanently
Removes the need for any taxation
Guarantees the budget is always balanced
Reduces the temptation to create inflation for short term political gain
questions.explanation: An independent central bank is insulated from short term political pressure, making a low inflation commitment more credible. This helps anchor expectations and keep inflation under control.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: During a deep recession with very low interest rates, some economists argue fiscal policy is more effective than monetary policy because what may happen?
options: The money supply is fixed by law
Government spending has no effect on demand
Interest rates cannot fall much further to stimulate spending
Taxes can no longer be changed
questions.explanation: When interest rates are already near zero, the central bank has little room to cut them further, so monetary policy loses traction. Direct government spending and tax changes can then be a more reliable way to lift demand.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
